Title: GTC preview: Inside the AI factory — The $1T infrastructure war under the hood of the AI economy
URL: https://siliconangle.com/2026/03/14/gtc-preview-inside-ai-factory-1t-infrastructure-war-hood-ai-economy/
Time Published: 2026-03-15T03:01:34Z
Full Content:
UPDATED 23:01 EDT / MARCH 14 2026 by John Furrier Every year the artificial intelligence industry gathers at Nvidia Corp.’s GPU Technology Conference expecting to see faster graphics processing units, bigger models and the next wave of AI software innovation. And yes, that will all be there. But if you’ve been watching the conversations we’ve been having on theCUBE over the past few years, you know the real story isn’t just happening on stage — it’s happening under the hood. What’s unfolding right now is the largest infrastructure buildout the tech industry has seen since the birth of the cloud. But unlike the cloud era, which was largely a software revolution, the AI era is rapidly becoming something else entirely. It’s becoming industrial. The companies racing to lead this new era aren’t just writing code. They’re securing power, booking semiconductor capacity, locking in memory supply and deploying massive clusters designed to produce intelligence at scale. We call this system the AI factory. And as we head into GTC, the deeper narrative isn’t just about the next GPU architecture. It’s about a global infrastructure race — a trillion-dollar supply chain trench war — to build the factories that will manufacture intelligence for the next decade. What’s even more interesting is that this factory is no longer confined to hyperscale data centers. It’s beginning to extend outward into what we call the hyperconverged edge, where AI moves closer to where data is created and decisions are made. That shift — from centralized AI to a distributed network of intelligence factories — may ultimately define the next phase of the industry. Over the past few years we’ve been describing a shift in how computing infrastructure is designed. Traditional data centers stored data and ran applications. AI infrastructure does something fundamentally different: It manufactures intelligence. An AI factory is a vertically integrated system designed to convert raw inputs — power, silicon, memory and data — into outputs such as AI models, inference services, automation and reasoning systems. Companies leading this transformation include Nvidia, Amazon.com Inc., Microsoft Corp., Google LLC, Meta Platforms Inc., among others. Collectively, these firms are investing hundreds of billions of dollars in AI infrastructure. Some of our reported estimates suggest the next phase of this cycle could approach $1 trillion in capital investment. GTC will showcase the latest pieces of this architecture — but the most important story lies deeper in the supply chain. One of the strangest economic dynamics in this cycle is what I call the GPU appreciation paradox. In traditional technology markets, hardware depreciates rapidly. But in the AI era, the opposite appears to be happening. Take the widely deployed Nvidia H100 GPU. Instead of losing economic value over time, the productivity of these chips is increasing as the models they serve become more powerful. As frontier models improve, the value generated by the compute serving those models rises. This is why some AI labs are locking in multiyear GPU contracts at roughly $2.40 per hour, well above estimated build costs. AI compute has become the most constrained resource in the digital economy. And that’s turning GPUs into something closer to productive capital assets than traditional information technoogy hardware. GPUs may get the headlines at GTC, but the real bottleneck in the AI factory could be memory. Modern AI systems rely heavily on long-context reasoning, meaning models can process huge sequences of text, code and multimodal data. That capability requires massive amounts of high-bandwidth memory or HBM. HBM is physically expensive: Our data suggest that by 2026, as much as 30% of hyperscaler capital expenditures could go toward memory alone (drumroll: Wait for all the price increases for systems). This shift is already reshaping semiconductor allocation across the industry, with memory supply increasingly prioritized for AI infrastructure. One of the key constraints we’re watching closely is semiconductor fabrication capacity. Datacenter accelerators are now consuming a growing share of manufacturing capacity at TSMC. Suppliers like NVIDIA (GPUs), Broadcom (TPUs and custom AI silicon), and others are committing to far more aggressive volume growth than traditional consumer chip designers. At a high level, there are two major constraints in the semiconductor manufacturing process that matter for AI infrastructure: 1. Front-end capacity: the upstream wafer fabrication stage, where advanced logic process nodes produce the silicon and logic circuits on wafers. 2. Back-end capacity: sometimes referred to as the mid-end where advanced packaging technologies such as CoWoS (Chip-on-Wafer-on-Substrate) integrate the chips with high-bandwidth memory (HBM), substrates, and other components to create the final accelerator packages. Manufacturers like TSMC have to carefully balance both stages of the process. Last year the back-end packaging stage, especially CoWoS, was the biggest bottleneck. While still tight, the constraint is increasingly shifting toward the front-end wafer fabrication capacity. The bottom line: AI demand is exploding, but silicon production is struggling to keep pace. To understand the ultimate limit of AI scaling, you have to go even further upstream. The real gatekeeper in the semiconductor ecosystem is ASML Holding NV, the Dutch manufacturer of extreme ultraviolet lithography machines. These tools are required to produce the most advanced chips. Each machine costs more than $350 million, contains hundreds of thousands of components, and relies on a supply chain of thousands of specialized suppliers. More importantly, production is limited. ASML can manufacture roughly 70 to 100 extreme ultraviolet tools per year through the end of the decade. That output effectively caps how quickly the world can expand advanced semiconductor production. You can innovate software at exponential speed. But you cannot easily scale the industrial manufacturing systems that produce the silicon. Another key input to the AI factory is energy. Frontier AI clusters now require enormous amounts of electricity — often measured in gigawatts. Yet power isn’t necessarily a hard scaling limit. It’s a cost problem. To bypass grid constraints and multi-year permitting timelines, hyperscalers and AI labs are increasingly deploying behind-the-meter power systems. These include natural gas turbines, modular microgrids, fuel cells and factory-built data center modules. Even if energy costs increase, the economics still favor early deployment because the marginal value of frontier AI models is so high. In the AI race, speed often matters more than efficiency. While the hyperscale AI factory is grabbing headlines, another important shift is happening further out in the infrastructure stack. The AI factory is expanding to the edge. Enterprises are increasingly deploying localized AI systems to support real-time decision-making in environments such as: factories, hospitals, retail stores, logistics centers and smart cities. These environments require low latency, data sovereignty and continuous operation even when cloud connectivity is limited. This is where the hyperconverged edge enters the picture. Hyperconverged edge platforms collapse networking, compute, storage, security and AI inference into unified edge infrastructure. Instead of isolated devices, organizations deploy distributed mini AI factories capable of running localized inference and synchronizing with centralized AI clusters. In this architecture, hyperscale AI factories train models, while hyperconverged edge systems operate those models in the real world. This distributed model of intelligence production will likely be a major theme emerging from GTC discussions. The AI infrastructure race is also becoming deeply geopolitical. Governments around the world increasingly see AI capability as a matter of national sovereignty. Control over AI infrastructure means control over economic competitiveness, industrial automation, defense systems and national innovation capacity The United States currently leads in several key areas, including AI software ecosystems and access to advanced manufacturing through partners such as Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co. But other regions are moving quickly. China is pursuing full-stack vertical integration through companies such as Huawei, investing heavily in domestic chip design, memory production and sovereign AI infrastructure. Europe and Middle Eastern nations are also investing in sovereign compute capacity to ensure they are not dependent on foreign cloud providers. In many ways, AI factories are becoming the strategic infrastructure of the digital age, similar to energy grids or telecommunications networks. Perhaps the biggest misconception about the AI boom is how it is categorized. Many investors still treat AI companies as traditional software firms. But the companies leading the AI Factory buildout increasingly resemble heavy industrial operators. They are deploying gigawatt-scale data centers, global semiconductor supply chains, massive capital investment programs and vertically integrated infrastructure stacks This is not just another software cycle. It’s the emergence of industrial-scale intelligence production. As we head into another Nvidia GTC, it’s easy to get caught up in the product announcements like the next GPU, the next model, the next software framework. But the bigger story unfolding beneath the surface is about infrastructure power. The companies that win the next four to five years in AI won’t simply be the ones with the best algorithms. They’ll be the ones that secured the supply chain early: The ones that are locked in the lithography capacity. The ones that booked the memory wafers. The ones that built the power plants. The ones that extended their AI factories all the way out to the edge. Because the future of AI is no longer just about training bigger models. It’s about building the global system that runs them. The AI factory is quickly becoming the industrial backbone of the digital economy — a distributed network of hyperscale clusters and hyperconverged edge infrastructure that together produce and operate intelligence. And if the last decade belonged to the cloud, the next decade will belong to the companies that build and control these factories. That’s the story I’ll be watching closely this week at GTC. Because the race to build the AI factory is only just getting started. Support our mission to keep content open and free by engaging with theCUBE community. Title: The Washington Post Lost Over 60,000 Subscribers Following Winter Layoffs
URL: https://www.thewrap.com/media-platforms/journalism/the-washington-post-lost-60000-subscribers-layoffs-jeff-bezos/
Time Published: 2026-03-14T23:41:05Z
Full Content:
The New York Times also reports executive editor Matt Murray had plans to leave the newspaper ahead of the cuts The Washington Post lost over 60,000 subscribers following Jeff Bezos’ sweeping layoffs of 44% of the paper’s newsroom in February, The New York Times reported Saturday. The Times additionally detailed “how Bezos upended the Washington Post,” per its headline, including his decision to retain executive editor Matt Murray on staff to see through the layoffs, even after CEO Will Lewis’ departure. The Times also noted a “spokesperson for the Post disputed” the 60,000 figure, “but declined to provide an alternate number.” Murray had known the layoffs of 350 staffers in February were coming since at least November. Murray also told Lewis he was planning to leave, according to The Times. Bezos stepped in and presented a plan: “Reduce the newsroom’s budget by half and double the productivity of those who remained, all while protecting some core parts of The Post’s coverage, like investigative journalism. Mr. Murray said he would take on that challenge,” the Times reported. The story comes as Bezos is set to have met with the newspaper’s masthead and some reporters Thursday. The Amazon founder has made little public comment since his initial Feb. 7 statement following the layoffs when he announced Lewis would depart the organization. Jeff D’Onofrio, the outlet’s chief financial officer, was named interim CEO and publisher. At the time, Bezos said the Post “has an essential journalistic mission and an extraordinary opportunity.” He did not mention Lewis. “Each and every day our readers give us a roadmap to success. The data tells us what is valuable and where to focus,” Bezos added. “Jeff, along with Matt and Adam, are positioned to lead The Post into an exciting and thriving next chapter.” Murray described the layoffs as a “strategic reset” at the time.
--------------------------------------------------

Title: How I Shot That: The Cameras and Lenses Behind the 2026 SXSW Features
URL: https://www.indiewire.com/gallery/sxsw-2026-feature-film-camera-survey/
Time Published: 2026-03-14T19:30:20Z
Full Content:
By providing your information, you agree to our Terms of Use and our Privacy Policy. We use vendors that may also process your information to help provide our services. This site is protected by reCAPTCHA Enterprise and the Google Privacy Policy and Terms of Service apply. Throughout the year, IndieWire asks cinematographers with films premiering at major festivals to tell us what formats, cameras, and lenses they chose in order to create the look of their films, and how those choices helped them craft visual worlds that range from the gritty to the grandiose. The point is never to count the number of ALEXA 35s or Sony Venice 2s, nor to acknowledge how nice it would be to shoot on film if the budget allows. It’s to see how cinematographers get inventive, solve problems, and capture a protagonist’s inner-world or the feeling of being in a specific place and time through the imagery we see. If there’s a running theme among the narrative features playing at the 2026 SXSW festival, it’s scrappy cinematography. Sometimes this is quite literal. Danny Madden, the director and director of photography on “Downbeat,” told IndieWire that since the main conceit of his film involved making art with trash, it only made sense to shoot the movie with a Handycam. “It brings a kind of raw access that felt correct for this movie about a feral guy tumbling his way through a dirty city. Shooting on a camcorder is also sort of spiritually linked to the story — make with what you’ve got — and I had a $900 used camera I bought with Kickstarter money,” Madden said. Likewise, Gemma Doll-Grossman, the cinematographer of “Sender,” told IndieWire that the key element dictating the look of the film was cardboard boxes. “The camera remains stubbornly static (No pans! No tilts!), unless it’s rigged to an everyday object — a tube of lipstick, a pill bottle, a cardboard box — which offers the impression of surveillance,” Doll-Grossman said. “Most of the film is captured with 12mm and 18.5mm lenses, which emulated a security camera’s perspective and gave our protagonist a wide canvas to play. And, undoubtedly, our aspect ratio is boxy 3:2.” But scrappiness has a wide range of meanings. DP Bella Gonzales refused to be satisfied with the tropes of drug trip imagery for “Pizza Movie,” and spent months with her business partner Andrew Laboy researching and developing handmade custom filters. “We experimented with hundreds of materials — often layering and combining them — to create effects that felt original, tactile, and slightly surreal. To push those filters further, my camera assistants Michael “Moose” Belardi and Adam Gonzalez worked with gaffer Kevin Kim to build a custom matte box that allowed us to interact with the filters using external light. That flexibility gave us the ability to shape and activate the textures in camera, creating a visual language that felt playful, handcrafted, and specific to the film,” Gonzales told IndieWire. Read the answers below to see how each of these 50 cinematographers created a visual language specific to their film. Dir: John Valley, DP: Raylor Camarot Category: Midnighter Format: 6k Opengate X-OCN Camera: Sony Venice 1, FX3 Lenses: Zeiss Nano primes, Fujinon Premista 28-100 The Look: We tested a couple camera and lens options — at one point we even considered shooting super16 crop mode on an Alexa (almost polar opposite look). We ended up going with the Venice and Nano primes because they gave us a ‘medium format’ feel. Something about the square sensor and shallow depth of field resonated with us, allowing us to guide the audience’s attention without being heavy-handed visually. The Nano’s are sharp wide open, so even our wide shots have separation of focus. We chose 4:3 because we were shooting a lot of houses (both real and doll-sized), so a squarish aspect ratio just felt natural. Plus, I’ve always loved framing people in 4:3. There are a few tracking shots that we shot with an FX3 on a Ronin, only because that was what we could afford. We used the Premista zoom for very specific shots, but we only had it for the few days we needed it. Which was a blessing and a curse, John and I inevitably fall in love with a zoom every time we have one. John and I’s work has a distinct look, no matter how much we try and fight it. Eventually, instinct takes over, and our tastes align in a way that creates something slightly ‘off kilter’. It’s hard to put into words, but it’s inherently rough around the edges. Like the image is barely holding itself together. For this film, that tension was perfect. Dir & DP: Polaris Banks Category: Visions Format: 3.4K ARRIRAW Open Gate, 720p H.264, 1080p Full HD MPEG-4 Camera: ALEXA Mini, iPhone, Canon EOS 70D Lenses: Arri Master Primes, Angenieux Optimo DP Zooms, iPhone Lens (No Adapter) The Look: “And Her Body Was Never Found” is about a couple making a movie in the wilderness all by themselves. So we were restricted to static two shots and moving singles operated by each other, with absolutely no focus pulling. In spite of the limitations, we wanted to push what could be accomplished by only two people. So we bought a used ALEXA Mini and found a private owner willing to cut us a deal on some Master Primes, which would bring the wow factor just sitting on a tripod. We needed to carry the entire production on our backs for hours of hiking a day, though. So I limited our set to the 50mm, 35mm, and 18mm, which were still brutally heavy when added to the camera body and batteries. After two trips out, the rental and insurance were costing too much. So I had to switch to a pair of Angenieux Optimo DP zooms my brother owns to finish out the Alexa section. We also used a Tiffen softnet black 1 filter to take the digital edge off, but I probably should’ve gotten a pearlescent filter too, because you can see the net structure sometimes in the bokeh. Halfway through the narrative, we pivot to shooting on iPhones, which completely revitalized the up to that point grueling production. Mor reveled in adding little zooms and well-timed whip pans to punctuate the comedy and terror, really blurring the line between performance and cinematography. Instead of being locked into strict marks like the first leg of shooting, we chased each other all over the locations and let the autofocus do its thing. The freedom and joy really bleeds into those scenes. For the interview clip at the end, we rented five Canon 70Ds from five different owners off ShareGrid, which took us all day to pick up from every corner of LA. I had to pop up between each take, cut all five cameras, give notes, re-roll all five, then sit back down to act. It was a little silly, but having five angles was such a luxury to edit. Dir: Brian Tetsuro Ivie, DP: Matheus Bastos Category: Spotlight Format: 16mm Film Camera: Arriflex 416 Lenses: Cooke S4X + SK4 Primes, Cooke Varokinetal 10.4-52mm S16 Zoom The Look: “Anima” is a sci-fi road trip film that tells a cross-cultural and cross-generational story about a young woman embarking on a road trip with an old, wealthy businessman to preserve his consciousness at an experimental facility. We were equally inspired by both independent American and Japanese cinema from the ‘90s and early 2000s, but in a context of futuristic (or modern-day…) developments in technology, where that meets us and our humanity, and the ethics that lie in between. For that reason, we also wanted to give the film a handmade and textured look, but still have flavors of a classically shot film that you might’ve found on the shelf of a Blockbuster back in the day. We knew that the film was going to be shot on Kodak 16mm film for this very reason, and my mind immediately went to lensing the film with Cooke lenses for their warmth, clarity, and familiar look across the history of cinema. We specifically used the Arri Rental modified Cooke S4X (originally made for “The Last of Us” Season 2) because I loved having the modified round aperture and faster T-Stop in most of our lenses. For our wide-angle options, we used the Cooke SK4 lenses, which are specifically made for S16 capture. I also mixed in my personal Cooke Varokinetal 10.4-52mm S16 zoom lens, which feels like the perfect blend between a Cooke Speed Panchro and a Cooke S4. Anima was shot on 3 film stocks: 7203 50D, 7213 200T, and 7219 500T. The entire film was overexposed 1 stop for a clean and rich negative. We scanned with Kodak NYC in 4K on a Lasergraphics Scantation. Huge shout-out to director Brian Tetsuro Ivie for being the co-founder of KEBRADO, a celluloid-only production company developing, producing, and shooting projects exclusively on 16mm, 35mm, and 65mm film. Thank you to Arri Rental for the support on the film as well! Dir: Chelsea Devantez, DP: Veronica Bouza Category: Spotlight Format: 4.6K Prores 4444 Camera: ALEXA 35 Lenses: Arri Signature Primes and Zooms The Look: “Basic” is a romantic comedy that dives into the insecurities in all of us. Visually, Director Chelsea Devantez and I wanted to create an elevated and modern look that paid homage to 2000s rom-coms. The ALEXA 35, paired with Arri Signature Primes and Zooms, gave us a beautiful balance of exceptional dynamic range and rich color rendition. For moments of heightened reality, we leaned more into a beauty look by glowing the highlights, adding a ½ Glimmer Glass. Dir: Will Ropp, DP: Matthew Pothier Category: Competition Format: Open gate ARRIRAW Camera: ALEXA 35 Lenses: Panavision Primos and Ultra Speeds The Look: The lenses are not too out of the ordinary. There are some Panavision Ultra Speeds I love, so I got those, and I liked the primos as a base set since they didn’t call too much attention to themselves. We did stick to longer focal lengths for the most part to give it a slightly more isolated feel compared to a typical comedy shot on all wides. I didn’t want to rely too much on adding optical distortion to bring in texture, but to do it through lighting techniques. A big part of the look was not changing the white balance of the camera; we kept it set at 5600k as if it were a film stock and made all the adjustments with old-school color correction filters. We also only used tungsten lights as well to enhance that look. This was a technique I was experimenting with on some commercials that summer, and when we tested it during prep, it seemed to add some really interesting tonality to [Ben Wang’s] midtones and skin, so we went with it! Dir: Jonás Cuarón, DP: Pepe Ávila del Pino Category: Spotlight Format: ARRIRAW Camera: ALEXA 35 Lenses: Mini Cooke s4, White Point, Canon zoom 8-64mm The Look: The sensor of the ALEXA 35 in combination with the optics of the Cooke, the white point, and the Canon zoom gave us the opportunity to render the colors and textures of the made-up world of Campeón. It also gave us the dynamic range and the information and detail needed for the work on VFX. Dir: Marq Evans, DP: Jason Roark Category: Spotlight Format: 4K, 6K Braw 16:9 Camera: Blackmagic P6k Pro, Blackmagic P4k, Canon 814 Super 8mm Lenses: Veydra Mini Cinema Primes The Look: Production for “Capturing Bigfoot” was an interesting mix of scheduled, 3-camera interviews and a lot of “catch what you can” verité. We often followed our colorful characters into unknown circumstances, and the small format of my Blackmagic cameras was key to being able to move quickly and shoot high-quality images from multiple angles. We’d often have one camera on sticks or a monopod, and another on a gimbal. I had great support from Erik Wallin and Gregg Frost when shooting different angles, and Erik shot some absolutely beautiful drone footage. Early in production, Director Marq Evans and I decided to use the Blackmagic cameras that I own. I love to rent an Alexa when I can, but having the kit ready to go at a moment’s notice was valuable. Marq teaches Documentary Filmmaking at Olympic College in Washington State, and is a great editor, fluent in the nuts and bolts of production, and the Braw Codec is solid and easy to work with. I love the Blackmagic Cameras for their image quality and small form factor, and my Veydra Mini Primes on the Pocket 4ks are gorgeous — so great for interviews. The Blackmagic cameras are small enough to easily pack for travel (there was a lot), and work well on the Ronin. I shot some of our recreation sequences on my trusty Canon Auto Zoom 814 Super 8. I’ve had it since college (found in a thrift store for $12 if I remember right!) and always love to get it in the mix. For recreations from the 1960’s, it felt perfect. Dir: Edd Benda & Stephen Helstad, DP: Cristina Dunlap Category: Spotlight Format: ProRes 4444XQ Camera: ALEXA 35 Lenses: Konica Hexanons, Angenieux Optimo 24-290 The Look: Stephen, Edd, and I were fortunate to do extensive lens testing with one of our actors before heading out to shoot in Illinois. From the outset, we knew we wanted to work with the ALEXA 35, taking advantage of its low-light sensor, as we would be shooting in rural Illinois — an area known for being a “dark zone” with little to no ambient light at night. The ALEXA 35 allowed us to hold detail deep into the shadows while preserving highlight information, giving us the flexibility to let darkness exist naturally in the frame without sacrificing texture. After evaluating how each lens set rendered color, the Konica Hexanons became the unanimous choice. We were drawn to their cooler tonal quality to counterbalance the heavy warmth of our humid Midwestern summer locations — the kind of climate that naturally amplifies warm hues, especially in skin tones. We also knew we’d be staging a large practical fire in a pivotal scene and wanted to avoid an image that felt washed out or overwhelmingly warm. The Konicas are vivid, punchy lenses with strong natural contrast and beautifully vibrant greens and blues. They proved to be the perfect complement to both our locations and our evolving visual language. Part thriller, part dark comedy, we wanted the look of the film to shift as chaos descends upon the characters. It begins as a softer family dramedy and gradually transforms into something far more stark and high-contrast — mirroring the internal unraveling of our protagonists. We also incorporated the Angénieux Optimo 24–290 to convey moments when the world feels as though it’s closing in on Jess, our main character. The lens allowed us to subtly compress space and intensify that psychological pressure without disrupting the film’s visual continuity. Dir: Dempsey Bryk, DP: Kristofer Bonnell Category: Spotlight Format: ARRIRAW 4.6K Open Gate Camera: ALEXA 35 Lenses: Zeiss Super Speed / Standard Speed The Look: When thinking about what lens and camera package to use for this film, Dempsey Bryk and I both knew early on that a lot of it would be shot handheld, and the takes would be quite long at times. Our locations were beautiful, but they carried a sense of wear and a subtle brokenness to them. After testing a handful of different lenses, we landed on the Super Speeds. Not only because they are small and fast, but because we also were looking for lenses that had a nice balance of vintage characteristics while still remaining sharp enough wide open when needed in low light. The lower contrast and delicate fall-off helped match the emotional tone of the film, embracing both the beauty and imperfection of the spaces. When pairing them with the standard speeds, it gave us a great range of focal lengths, giving us ultimate creative flexibility. The other lens in our kit, which we used for a number of scenes, was the 25-250 Angenieux HR. Having the zoom in the kit allowed us to take a step back and create an alternate perspective when we wanted to watch from an objective point of view, create distance between the characters, or add any immersive zooms. Shooting on the Alexa 35 was an easy choice. Aside from the unmatched colour science, beautifully accurate skin tones, and film-like texture, we had a lot of high noon sun and night scenes to contend with. I knew I was going to need the strong dynamic range of the camera on both ends of the scale. As we watched back dailies each day and later entered the post-production phase, it proved to be the right choice. Dir: Jo Rochelle, DP: Anup Kulkarni Category: Spotlight Format: 4K Ultra HD, Log C, QuickTime, Apple ProRes 4444, 2.39:1 aspect ratio Camera: ALEXA Mini, ALEXA Mini LF Lenses: ZEISS Compact Prime- CP.3 The Look: For “Dead Deer High,” the cinematography had to serve vulnerability above all else. This is a story about young poets navigating grief and rediscovering their voices. The camera needed to feel present and intimate, but never intrusive. It had to honor performance, hold space for silence, and respond truthfully to natural light. The ALEXA Mini LF was the right tool because it gave us both emotional depth and physical flexibility. The large-format sensor produces a subtle dimensionality that makes faces feel sculptural and grounded in space. In a film centered on spoken word, micro-expressions matter. The way the sensor renders skin tone, highlight roll-off, and color separation allowed us to capture those nuances without exaggeration. We often worked in available light or lightly shaped practical environments, and the camera’s dynamic range let us embrace shadow and softness without introducing noise or losing tonal integrity. Equally important was the camera’s compact, modular design. Much of the film takes place in real classrooms and rehearsal spaces. I needed to move fluidly in tight environments and operate close to the actors to preserve immediacy. I also built custom lens attachments for several experimental POV sequences. The lightweight body allowed us to execute those moments without technical constraints, keeping the emotional continuity intact. I paired the camera with Zeiss CP.3 primes because of their balance between clarity and character. The CP.3 lenses are sharp, but not sterile. They offer controlled contrast and a subtle organic texture that supported the film’s grounded tone. Their consistency across focal lengths helped maintain visual cohesion as we shifted from ensemble classroom scenes to intimate close-ups during performances. Together, this combination gave us a visual language. The tools disappeared, allowing the poetry, the performances, and the healing journey to remain at the forefront. Dir: Richard E. Williams, DP: Julian Panetta Category: Visions Format: 6k OCN XT Camera: Sony Venice Rialto Lenses: Custom-tuned Panavision H series 20mm The Look: The film’s unique, first-person perspective dictated a system that was not only lightweight but also as physically short as possible. The shortness is important for any hand acting from our operator, as well as getting the other characters to really get in the audience’s face when we needed to. The Rialto gave us all the usability and expected quality of a cinema camera, but in the form of a tiny body. We used a helmet rig, which ended up being surprisingly ergonomic and agile, however we put the Venice body on a backpack mount, which was worn by another crew member, so there was an added challenge of choreographing another crew member to stay close to the operator and stay out of shot, too. Richard loves the K35’s, but from an efficiency standpoint, why rent the whole set if you’re only going to use one lens? That’s part of why I love going to Panavision, you only need to hire what you’re actually going to use. Being able to have our optics custom-tuned meant we could have something that met the needs of the job, like great minimum focus and a small footprint with a lot of field curvature, halation, flares, and veiling glare, which directly reinforces the instability of Sean’s perspective. Its very easy to get caught up in figuring out what is a “realistic” field of view for humans, and truth be told its certainly a lot wider than a 20mm lens, but opting for something in that range allows us to be a lot more selective about what’s in and out of the frame, and also doesn’t overly distort close ups. All of this combined destabilises the image enough to keep it feeling subjective, not like Meta Glasses, which are just recording what’s in front of them. Dir & DP: Danny Madden Category: Spotlight Format: HD, XAVC Camera: Sony Handycam AX-100 Lenses: That little Zeiss zoomer built into the camera The Look: The joke was: This is a movie about a guy making art with trash, let’s do the same with the filmmaking. In truth, though, I love the look of the Handycam. It brings a kind of raw access that felt correct for this movie about a feral guy tumbling his way through a dirty city. Shooting on a camcorder is also sort of spiritually linked to the story — make with what you’ve got — and I had a $900 used camera I bought with Kickstarter money. This was my first film as cinematographer, but most of the time I operate the camera when I direct. On other films, even with an Alexa Mini stripped down as much as we could manage, I never felt that I could get at the agility I wanted, the kind of freedom of movement I had shooting as a teenager on a Canon GL1. That top-grip skate video kind of motion, lightness, and swiftness of process. This camera did it, and even though it shoots H264 we made it work on the back end. A shout-out to the film’s beloved colorist Sean Wells at Roast n’ Post in Oakland! As I sent him camera tests in pre-production, he was very supportive of the look of it, limitations and all. “Just get it as close as you can on the day,” he told me. That was excellent advice and a confidence-giving endorsement. Dirs: Raviv Ullman & Greg Yagolnitzer, DP: Ben Goodman Category: Midnighter Format: X-OCN ST (8.6K) Camera: Sony Venice 2 Lenses: ARRI Signature Primes and Zooms The Look: “Drag” unfolds over the course of a single night in one location. The central challenge in developing the look of the film was introducing visual variety without hindering cohesiveness or continuity. From early conversations with writers/directors Raviv Ullman and Greg Yagolnitzer, I knew the imagery would be bold. Some scenes take place in unexpectedly vibrant rooms filled with saturated artwork and expressive production design. Others occur in near-total darkness, where we avoided artificial “moonlight” through windows and instead sought to evoke the sensation of one’s eyes adapting to low-level ambience. To meet these and other demands, I chose to work with the Sony VENICE 2. I shot the entire film at its higher base ISO of 3200 with a 1-stop pulldown LUT, rating my meter at 1600 EI. This approach gave us substantial latitude in grading the film’s darkest scenes without introducing unwanted noise. It also allowed me to maintain a working stop of around T2.8 while lighting large background areas with a relatively modest package. The 8.6K resolution offered valuable compositional flexibility. I shot in the 17:9 mode framed for a 2:1 extraction with 5% safety on all sides, which gave us plenty of room to refine framing and occasionally stabilize shots in post. With generous support from ARRI Rental, we carried two complete camera packages: One permanently built for Steadicam and the other with the Rialto Extension System. The film employs graphic, unconventional perspectives — often in confined spaces — and our schedule required rapid transitions between handheld, dolly, crane, low-angle prism, and even a tree mount, among other configurations. The Rialto introduced versatility and efficiency without sacrificing image quality. I paired the VENICE 2 with ARRI Signature Primes, which I find strike a balance of technical precision and pleasing character. Their consistency across the full range of focal lengths unified the frames of the film. And critically, the wider lenses didn’t introduce distracting or unfavorable distortion. I must also say that while these tools were essential in making “Drag,” none of it would have been possible without our exceptional crew across all departments. Dir: Alex Prager, DP: Lol Crawley Category: Spotlight Camera: Sony Venice Lenses: Baltar The Look: An accomplished photographer asking a cinematographer to shoot for them is a huge compliment, but potentially a unique challenge in that you have two image makers on set. Alex made it very clear from the outset that she was looking for a collaborator, not just a mimic for her own aesthetic. Recognising that the majority of my work was grounded in a more naturalistic and often ‘caught’ aesthetic she joked that I could bring the spirit of Robert Altman to her appreciation of Hitchcock and Douglas Sirk. Alex had already spent weeks drawing her own storyboards which was vital in terms of conveying her intended coverage and visual language for each scene but she was also entirely willing to build on these ideas and we had a wonderful time shot listing at her studio, acting out the scenes together and honing the visual language of the film. I have shot many feature films on 35mm, including the VistaVision camera system, and Alex shares my love for the celluloid aesthetic. Unfortunately, it was not possible to shoot “DreamQuil” on film (aside from shooting the miniatures in VistaVision), and so in collaboration with Picture Shop LA, we started to explore the ways in which we could make our Sony Venice digital camera closely emulate the look of 35mm. Our final DI colorist, Tim Stipan, along with his Picture Shop colleagues, worked tirelessly to create our show LUT, emphasizing the saturation of the reds and other key colours integral to the production, costume, and makeup design, whilst keeping the remaining colour palette a soft pastel. After thorough testing, we chose to shoot on a combination of older Baltar and Kowa prime lenses combined with A, B & C Mitchell diffusion filters to reduce the ultra-sharp definition and detail level of modern cinema cameras. We used Mole-Richardson lights to bring yet a further classical cinema feel. Dirs: Megan Rico and Kade Atwood, DP: Brenna Empey Category: Competition Format: XAVC-I 4K Camera: Sony FX6 Lenses: Tokina Zooms The Look: Our approach to “Edie Arnold’s” cinematography was a loving embrace of scrappiness and speed. Principal photography was ten days with very limited resources and budget, which meant I sought out a camera that I’d normally bring onto a documentary project — the Sony FX6. Its versatility, featherweight body, and stellar performance in low light (dual ISO till I die!) fit our needs to a T. These limitations also forced us to get hyper-strategic with our shot list. It was all about moving quickly without sacrificing style or comedic timing, which resulted in directors Kade and Megan and I designing many locked-off, scene-encompassing tableaus for the characters to romp around in, in lieu of relying on traditional coverage, which would’ve put too much pressure on our already challenging schedule. I think our choreographed wides really shine because of this planning. Some days, there was little-to-no camera operating on my end — the directors and I talked more than once about this approach, making some shots “painterly”. And when you’re only given ten days to shoot a feature, it’s time to break out not only the Sachtler Flowtech sticks but also a set of zooms, in our case, the Tokina 25-75mm and 50-135mm. They lean cool-toned, which I find gorgeous. Typically, I’ll reach for a set with a wider stop, but the physical and visual comedy in this film meant opening up beyond a T/2.8 (and thereby letting the background fall into mush) never quite served the jokes we were trying to tell. We rarely ventured wider than an T/4. Yes, my 1st AC was in heaven. Dirs: Kevin Bacon and Kyra Sedgwick, DP: Mac Fisken Category: Spotlight Format: Prores 4k Camera: DJI Ronin 4D-8k Lenses: Sirui Saturn 1.6x Anamorphic The Look: When Kyra and Kevin first described this project with so many characters, animals, spontaneity, and a lot of movement, I immediately felt like the 4D would be a great fit. It allowed us to work fast and flexibly, running around the large environment without a big footprint but still moving with the actors in a way that felt very intimate. When Phil the goat moved the wrong way, we could adjust on the fly and get a push in that captured the moment and still felt cinematic. We shot everything on location, so the camera’s ability to jam into a corner of a tiny bathroom or pass through a window meant we could get shots that I’ve never been able to get before. Ultimately, it was a tool that freed us up to play with the shots — crucial for a movie about the joy of indie filmmaking. Dir: Meredith Alloway, DP: Karim Hussain Category: Spotlight Format: 4K ProRes 4444 XQ, LOG C4, Arri Texture G733 Nostalgic Camera: ALEXA 35 Lenses: Canon K-35, Canon FD, Angenieux 25-250 Original Vintage Zoom The Look: “Forbidden Fruits” needed a softer patina to the image, a dreamy and 1960s/1970s Giallo vibe to counter the crass modern cleanliness of a functioning mall. The women had to look iconic. We didn’t want to use excessively heavy diffusion that would make the practicals bloom too much, so we chose Canon K-35 primes and shot them all wide open, which gave a very magical glow to the images naturally. For a zoom, we used a 1970 original Angenieux 25-250 zoom nicknamed “Lucky Pierre,” which has fungus in his rear element, but really adds a unique feeling to images because of that. We shot with the Arri Alexa 35 camera, baking in the G733 Nostalgic texture into the image, which gave a beautiful, painterly, and extremely filmic texture to everything. All shots were done at 2000 ISO to organically create the grain, as the Alexa 35 reacts to light in a similar manner as film stock, only with greater latitude. I used the Cinefade on every shot as a rapid remote variable ND, to swiftly balance both A+B cameras, while keeping the different speed lenses on them all wide open. I have used this technique on multiple projects before, and it’s always been a great time saver. It can have a slight green cast that you can dial out if desired, but you have to use it precisely to not get vignetting on the edges of the frame if you go beyond ND1.8. The color scheme of the movie and lighting was meticulously planned out in advance, following the narrative flow, starting warmer then progressively getting colder in terms of color. This was done in camera using color temperature settings, then augmented in the final color grade with Jim Fleming at Picture Shop. The lighting oscillates between an adapted version of the bright, practical mall lighting that we tweaked, to much more shadowy, stylish light during the Paradise rituals and the climax. Shots done through two-way mirrors also added infinity reflection effects for the changing room confessionals, to illustrate the multi-layers of emotions these women are feeling. Dir: Dario Russo, DP: Matthew Chuang Category: Spotlight Format: 4.6K Arriraw Camera: ALEXA 35 Lenses: Panavision Primo primes and zooms The Look: Director Dario Russo wanted a very ’70s approach to the visual style with no moving camera and very minimal coverage. We chose to go with the ALEXA 35 and primarily the Primo 27mm as it framed our actors within the space in a way that felt right to us. Part of the ’70s feel we captured scenes with only a slow zoom. The Primo 19-90mm gave us the range we needed for those moments. Dirs: Ed Dougherty and Brea Grant, DPs: Alexander Chinnici, Mark Evans, Yaron Levy, Taylor Washington Category: Midnighter Format: 2.8K ARRIRAW Camera: ALEXA 35 Lenses: K 35’s for Content Moderation and Optimo Zooms for Union Meeting The Look: We chose the K35’s for their color rendering and softness. “Content Moderation” is all about building the tension and world of the pit as the main character is driven mad, and these lenses were perfect for giving us our unique looks. (I was also inspired by their use on “Aliens”) The Optimos were perfect for our budget and “Succession”-esque filming style while keeping us quick on our feet to get the coverage Ed needed for the union meeting. We wanted each segment to feel very different, so we aimed to use varying lenses and visual language. For “Content Moderation,” we utilized Sticks/fluid movement on primes to portray the claustrophobia and madness of the world, and for “Union,” we stuck with handheld with zooms for the fun chaos/action of our ensemble cast. — Taylor Washington Dir: Drew Kirsch, DP: Brett Pawlak Category: Spotlight Format: 4K Pro Res 4444 Camera: ALEXA 35 Lenses: K-35 ‘s The Look: For “He Bled Neon,” we chose the ALEXA 35 paired with vintage K-35 lenses, a combination that gave us the flexibility of a modern digital camera while retaining some of the character of older glass. The story unfolds across two aspect ratios that mirror the emotional arc of the character. When the frame expands into the taller 1.43 format, we utilized the full Super 35 sensor, allowing the image to feel more immersive while giving us the latitude to hold detail in both highlights and color. Lighting was approached in a very practical way. Because of the camera’s sensitivity, I relied heavily on real practical fixtures to motivate and often provide the majority of the light in the scene. Working closely with production designer Carlos Lazlo, we designed many of these practicals directly into the sets so that the lighting could live naturally within the world of the film. It became a true collaboration between cinematography and production design, where lamps, fixtures, and architectural elements were doing as much storytelling as traditional film lights. Rather than leaning on lens artifacts like heavy flares or distortion, the visual identity of the film comes more from color and contrast. Together with colorist Ian Vertovec at Light Iron, we developed a custom LUT loosely inspired by Ektachrome. In the final grade, the image pushes toward bold color and aggressive contrast, with a texture that feels closer to a 35mm point-and-shoot photograph — slightly imperfect, tactile, and grounded in the world of the story. Dir: Caleb Phillips, DP: Allie Schultz Category: Midnighter Format: 4K ProRes 4444 Camera: ALEXA 35 Lenses: Cooke Panchro Classics The Look: “Imposters” is a mystery thriller that centers on the crumbling relationship of a couple after their son goes missing. The director, Caleb, and I opted for a naturalistic visual approach, grounding this story in the real world as much as possible. From early on, we both gravitated towards shooting spherical and cropping to a 2.39 aspect ratio. There’s a sense of realism and lack of artifice that comes with shooting spherical that was perfect for capturing the truth of these characters. We tested a variety of lenses and found the Cooke Panchro Classics were everything we were looking for. They fall into a sort of Goldilocks zone for softness and sharpness. There’s clarity, but they’re not so sharp that it looks clinical, and at wide open, focus falls off around the edges, giving dimensionality to the image in a really beautiful way. Also, I love that with these lenses you get the optical characteristics of a vintage lens, but with the mechanical benefits of a modern housing. Our schedule required us to move fast, and these lenses were super reliable to work with. I knew from the beginning that one of our biggest challenges would be shooting at night in the woods. The woods are a major character in this story, and the low light sensitivity of the ALEXA 35 was essential. For certain scenes, we wanted to light with mostly practical flashlights and lanterns that the actors would be carrying. I heavily utilized the enhanced sensitivity mode of the ALEXA 35, along with the deep shadow texture, to capture these scenes with minimal lighting. The incredible dynamic range of the ALEXA 35 made it possible for us to make our days amidst an intense schedule. Every second counted, and that meant sometimes there wasn’t enough time to net a window or control a highlight, but I never had to worry about the exposure clipping since I knew we had tons of flexibility in the image once we got to the color grade. Dir: Peter Warren, DP: Conor Murphy Category: Spotlight Format: 4.6K ProRes Camera: ALEXA 35 Lenses: Zeiss Super Speeds The Look: “Kill Me” is a mystery film, and Peter and I wanted the audience to discover that they could trust neither the characters nor the images we placed in front of them. We trained the camera alternatingly on the current timeline of the film, the fractured memories of the protagonist, and the evolving, competing theories that he entertains as he goes about his detective work. We considered a variety of visual tools to create clear distinctions between each of these perspectives, but in the end, we opted for only subtle color and lighting cues to set them apart and to instead leave room for uncertainty. We did use a distortion filter based on the deakinizer lens to depict a few especially immersive memories. We wanted to drown the audience in evidence and speculation, and the resulting daily shotlist was like nothing I had ever contended with before. While a laid-back shoot can be nice, the satisfaction of filming “Kill Me” was found instead in packing each day with Tetris-like efficiency and working our way through volumes of disembodied inserts without getting lost or snagged. The dense, fast-paced sequences that you see in the film are the result of substantial planning. We opted for Zeiss Super Speeds in order to shoot quickly in tight spaces, and the Alexa 35 for its range and filmic color space. We often used 4′ and 6′ sliders for short pushes, or to find shoulders and hug the line. There were times during the shoot that I found myself wishing for the conveniences of a stage, but when I watch the film, I can see that it thrives off of the claustrophobia that practical locations imposed on us. Dir: Arabella Oz, DP: Jeff Griecci Category: Competition Format: 4.6K 3:2 Open Gate, ProRes 4444 XQ Camera: ALEXA 35 Lenses: TLS Cooke Speed Panchro Primes & the Cooke Varotal Mkiii The Look: In my earliest conversations with Arabella and Nick for “Mallory’s Ghost,” they referenced classic comedies, both romantic and supernatural, spanning decades as far back as the 1940’s. I especially latched onto their inspirations from the 70’s, as I felt that era’s look best fit the overall tone of Arabella’s script. We leaned into that visual mindset. We knew that a filmic look over a modern digital glossiness would be the approach. We wanted a gentle vintage quality, something that would handle skin tones well and provide a generally warm feeling overall. It became obvious that the Cooke Speed Panchro primes would be the choice to pair with our Alexa 35 package. We added a Cooke 25-250mm Varotal Mkiii into the mix for our zoom shots. I remember on Day 1 of production, when we broke out the 100mm prime (which we almost passed on, including in the rental, before Arabella insisted). I saw just how remarkable the image looked when we opened up the lens for a portrait: a frame of the ghost character sitting in the garden, reflecting on her mortal self. The focus roll-off and the bokeh, with the lens wide open, was so striking. It gave just the right subtle amount of dreaminess we wanted to help distinguish the fantasy from reality. Shooting with rehoused primes from TLS and the Alexa 35 made the practicalities of the production a breeze. I think the vintage Cookes paired with that camera make for such a pleasing image. The combination of old glass and modern tech provides the best of both worlds; a really nice combination for indie productions. Dir: Nan Feix, DP: Matt Batchelor Category: Spotlight Format: Super 16mm Kodak Stock. 7219 500T and 7207 250D Camera: Arri 416 Lenses: Arri/Zeiss S16 Ultra Primes and Canon 8-64mm 2.4 The Look: For me, the approach to this film was all about simplifying things. Shooting a film that uses all live locations, almost all real people, and the unpredictability that comes with that requires you to be prepared for anything. Film was always part of the dogma and visual approach. The 416 is simply the best film camera ever made, in my opinion. There is so much life and texture in the neighborhoods of Chinatown, and 16mm was the format that best brought that life to the screen. The Ultra 16 lenses are predictable and sharp and allowed us to let the grit and texture come through the beautiful locations. Marni and the crew at Panavision NY were super helpful in making sure we had what we needed. We needed a format that allowed me to pull my own focus, operate in tight spaces, and react to what was happening around us. I couldn’t imagine using another format for this film. Our Director Nan Feix, Producer Marine Garnier, and Loveboat were all super supportive of shooting this film on celluloid. Dir: Chandler Levack, DP: Jeremy Cox Category: 24 Beats Per Second Format: ARRIRAW Camera: ALEXA 35 Lenses: Panavision G-Series Anamorphics The Look: In “Mile End Kicks,” Grace moves from her hometown to Montreal for the summer. We used anamorphic lenses to accentuate the feeling of seeing and experiencing something for the first time. Dirs: Matt Black and Ryan Polly, DP: Philip Lozano Category: Midnighter Format: 2,39:1 aspect ratio (anamorphic 2X desqueeze), 4K 6:5 XOCN XT recording Camera: Sony Venice 1, iPhone 15 PRO, MacBook Pro, Lipa HDV, GoPro Hero4, Webcam HP 320 FHD, Samsung mobile, Baby cam, PC laptop webcam, Sony nightvision handycam, Panasonic handycam, DJI Inspire 2. Lenses: Panavision T serie: 28/35/40/50/65/75/100/150. Panavision 90mm super macro. Panavision zoom 42-425 ALS10 The Look: With Matt & Ryan, we wanted the film to have a modern look, but personally, I hate modern sharp lenses. The Panavision T series and Sony Venice were the perfect combo — modern, with very few aberrations and with a subtle softness, giving a certain cinematic look, “out of reality” with the use of anamorphic lenses at the same time. We really wanted to embrace different looks shooting through many “cameras”, to show that the “disease” had spread over all devices and contents, as a metaphor for what happens in real life when people get sucked into scrolling and watching dumb content rather than connecting with other people. I liked that idea and really wanted to embrace it as much as we could, shooting with many devices/cameras/”shitty cams,” to give a certain visual richness through “rough looks” in addition to the elegance of our combo Sony Venice/T series. The cinematography tended to be natural but always stylized, and in such a horror genre movie I love to use lighting to show characters, or not — often silhouetting them, to narratively rise the level of fear of the audience, showing more or less of what they can or could see, or not, and always trying to bring depth and richness to the image. Dir: Dave Boyle, DP: Patrick Ouziel Category: Midnighter Format: 8.2K X-OCN LT and 4K XAVC S-I Camera: Sony Venice 2 and FX3 Lenses: Sigma FF High Speed Primes and Fujinon Cabrio 19-90mm The Look: In our early discussions, director Dave Boyle referenced films like “Don’t Look Now” (1973) and “The Changeling” (1980), and we were both interested in exploring a bit of a retro aesthetic. In practice, this meant embracing zooms, harder and less naturalistic lighting, as well as leaning into heavier haze use than I was accustomed to. My lighting director, Hidenori Nagata, was a master of using small mirrors to add interesting shapes all over the set. The mirrors also had a beautiful falloff when lighting faces. Our main camera package was a Venice 2, Sigma FF High Speed Primes, and a Fujinon Cabrio 19-90mm. We mostly used the 24mm, 35mm, and 50mm, because we like the feeling of presence you get when the camera is closer to the actor. The haze on set softened the contrast of our lenses and also added halation to windows and bright highlights. To punctuate certain psychologically intense moments for our main character, we used slow motion as well as a Laowa 24mm Pro2be, which allowed us to shoot from a unique perspective. The FX3 was used sparingly, mostly when the Venice was too big and heavy. We rigged it to a door and also sprinted with it for a “Raimi-cam” shot. I was so excited to work with Dave because he and I have very similar sensibilities when it comes to blocking and camera movement. Dave often describes our approach as “stately,” by which he means controlled dolly moves (sometimes combined with zooms) and elegant blocking that allows shots to develop in ways that engage an audience. For that reason, our Chapman Peewee III Plus (operated by my amazing dolly grip, Tsuneyuki Aiko) was also crucial. Camera placement is everything, and being able to quickly find exactly the right camera height for a shot is essential (not to mention being able to change it during a shot). Also, because I really favor Coen brothers-style top-down inserts, having a dolly makes these easily achievable. If I’m forced to pick between a more expensive camera and a dolly, the dolly wins every time. Dir: Ben Wheatley, DP: Armando Salas, ASC Category: Spotlight Format: REDCODE RAW Camera: Red Raptor XL[X] Lenses: Tribe7 Primes and Arri Signature Zooms The Look: Early in prep, director Ben Wheatley asked what an edgy version of “High Noon” shot in the 1970s during a blizzard might look like. That concept became the foundation of our visual approach. We created a film-emulation LUT with deep shadows, sharp contrast, and noticeable grain, paired with gentler glass to add subtle texture and personality — we wanted the image to feel tactile and weather-beaten. The softness of winter light, combined with atmospheric haze, allowed the shadows to open up in a natural way while still maintaining the grit we were after. The RED V-Raptor [X] was hugely beneficial for this approach because of its exceptional low-light sensitivity and color rendition. The global shutter was also key — it allowed us to capture strobing police lights and muzzle flashes in the middle of a blizzard without the distracting artifacts or broken frames that occur with rolling-shutter cameras. Dir: Jorma Taccone, DP: Matt Weston Category: Headliner Format: ARRIRAW Camera: ALEXA 35 Lenses: Hawk Class-X The Look: It’s not every day you get the opportunity to remake your own work. The whole experience was fascinating: Being able to watch the same story be brought to life by a whole different cast, in new locations, from a different director’s perspective. Again: Fascinating. Probably not unlike a sequel, we had a blueprint. The original (on which we used Mini LFs with Master anamorphics). A starting point from which we could ask ourselves what we liked and wanted to keep, and what could be improved upon. When selecting gear for a project, I think I’m always looking for a balance between character and practicality, within the bounds of the budget and what’s available to us. Arri cameras are always a solid foundation. I know what I’m able to get out of them. Easy to use. Reliable. They just stay out of the way and get the job done, allowing us to focus our energy on the rest of the process. The Hawks, I hadn’t had experience with before this. After a little testing and comparing, they just felt right. Hitting that balance. Interesting, but not distracting. I suppose we approached shooting the movie in much the same way. Finding the most interesting ways to tell the story without overcomplicating things and distracting from it. Dir: Millicent Hailes, DP: Ksusha Genenfeld Category: Visions Format: 4.6K ARRIRAW Camera: ALEXA 35 Lenses: Zeiss High Speeds and Angenieux Optimo 24-290 The Look: When I was first approached to shoot “Perfect,” our director Millicent Hailes wanted the world to feel raw, gritty, real, and textured. We both love the look of film and wanted the digital image to feel as close to that as possible. After testing a few options, we landed on the Zeiss High Speeds. Paired with the Alexa 35, these lenses gave us a slightly distorted yet clean image that felt grounded and real while still carrying a vintage quality. We wanted the lighting to feel very natural, almost effortless, but also dry and hot. The world we’re showing isn’t very comfortable to exist in. It’s summer, it’s sweaty, resources are limited, and the characters are young, messy, and still figuring themselves out. We wanted to lean into that unpolished feeling. Most of the film was shot handheld, which kept the camera feeling alive, and the compact size of the lenses made that approach possible. We also had many scenes that took place during blue hour, so having lenses that open up to T1.3 allowed us to keep shooting as long as possible as the light faded. These lenses were truly magical for us, and we couldn’t have done the project without the support of ARRI Rental. Millicent and I both love long takes that linger and build tension, so we occasionally used the 24–290 Optimo (thank you to my mentor Bill Bennett, ASC, for lending it to us). I’ve always been fascinated by shots where you don’t get to cut away, you stay with the actor, you live with them in the moment instead of cutting away. Some of our most emotional scenes work exactly like that: long zooms that slowly settle on the main character and allow the performance to breathe. This film felt like magic to make. It had the feeling of summer camp. We really got to make an honest movie, and mostly because I got to make it with my friends. Dir: Jake Kuhn and Noah Stratton-Twine, DP: Murray Zev Cohen Category: Visions Format: XAVC-I DCI 4K 422:HQ 10bit Camera: Sony FX6 Lenses: Sony 28-135mm F4 The Look: The Sony FX6 is a brilliant camera for smaller films like this. It’s more manageable for limited camera teams and multiple setups during the day. Shooting in 4k gave my director and editor the room to play with the footage through cropping where appropriate, and also having flexibility in the grade. This film was shot in 4:2:2 HQ 10-bit, which gave us very good information in the Raw files. Since this film was shot to be black-and-white, it was accompanied by a monochromatic LUT with a fairly high contrast. The camera was able to retain information in the shadows and highlights. I love the quick buttons on the FX6 and the customisable features. Anything to allow the day to flow better is always beneficial. The internal ND is fantastic for a camera with this price point, too. Paired with a circular polariser, it helped paint interesting images on the seashores of our locations. The single zoom lens was also ideal; the style of filmmaking for “Pincer Point” required several live zoom shots, so having the 28-135mm was a good, affordable option. A nicely timed zoom can really elevate tension and draw the audience in; finding that moment in each scene sometimes took a few takes. A zoom lens is also just more efficient for choosing a frame when under time constraints. However, this lens is fairly sharp, so I pretty much always place a BPM filter in front to take the edge off. The clarity this lens brings is still top-notch, so I find it quite reliable. Dir: Josh Plasse, DP: John Rosario Category: Spotlight Format: ARRIRAW 4.6K Camera: ALEXA 35 Lenses: Legacy Anamorphic 1.8x The Look: “The Pirate King” follows Todd Gillis (Rob Riggle), a down-and-out ex-Marine wrestling with trauma who slowly finds rehabilitation through an unlikely community of pirate reenactors. Director Josh Plasse and I set out to build a world that felt textured, raw, and authentic, grounded in reality but open to the subtle visual liberties offered by reenactment and Todd’s emotional progression. The ALEXA 35 was an obvious choice for me. Its dynamic range, resolution, and color science are well known, but what draws me to it is something simpler, more poetic. It is a reliable canvas, one that receives each stroke of light faithfully and with precision. The ability to add texture to the digital negative aligns with my philosophy of building the look of the film in camera as much as possible. Over time, it has become the most consistent and sharpest knife in my kit. I know its edge, how it carves light, renders a moment, and how to let it do its quiet work. The Legacy 1.8x anamorphic lenses developed by Stephen Gelb of Lensworks became central to the film’s visual language. They offered a rare combination of qualities that felt right for this world. I wanted character and distortion without distraction, a gentle falloff to isolate Todd within the frame and emphasize his loneliness, and expressive flares that echo the theatrical spirit of the pirate reenactments. Their compact size and minimal focus distance allowed us to remain nimble inside Todd’s small sailboat and close to the actors without relying on diopters. Todd’s world begins bleak and desaturated, almost suspended. As he encounters the pirates, warmth enters the frame through richer saturation, gold hues, and a subtle lingering red that hints at danger. Green, symbolizing renewal and healing, became a recurring motif we called Seaside Green, a custom gel combination developed with my gaffer Anthony Stivale and supported by a bespoke LUT crafted with colorist Nick Hasson of THE END. The look was a collective act, shaped by our crew, our tools, and the textures of Virginia Beach. In the end, everyone and everything had a hand in painting the image. Dirs: Nick Kocher and Brian McElhaney, DP: Bella Gonzales Category: Spotlight Format: 4.6k ProRes Camera: ALEXA 35 Lenses: Hawk V-Lite + Cooke Speed Panchros The Look: From the beginning, Nick and Brian were drawn to the texture and character of early-2000s comedies. While shooting on film was part of the early conversation, the realities of budget, schedule, and the fast-moving style they prefer on set made a digital workflow the more practical path. For that reason, I chose the ALEXA 35. One of the key considerations was avoiding the extremely shallow depth of field often associated with large-format sensors and instead maintaining an image closer to the feel of traditional 35mm photography. My goal was to shape a digital image that still carried a sense of photochemical texture. To help achieve that, we added grain in post and developed a custom LUT pipeline designed around a film-style tonal curve. We built the lighting strategy to feed into that curve so the image would respond with highlight roll-off and density reminiscent of film. Lens choice became the most important creative decision in achieving that visual language. Working closely with Keslow Camera, who are longtime collaborators of mine, I spent time testing what felt like nearly every lens option available. The film centers on a drug experience; it was important to clearly differentiate between the grounded reality of the story and the heightened perspective of the trip itself. That exploration led me to vintage spherical lenses, particularly the TCS-rehoused Cooke Speed Panchros, whose softer falloff and distinctive coatings paired beautifully with the Alexa 35. For the trip sequences, I leaned into something deliberately more “cinematic” in the most playful sense of the word. Filtration became another key part of that design. Rather than relying on conventional diffusion or post effects associated with drug imagery, my business partner, Andrew Laboy, and I spent months researching and developing handmade custom filters. We experimented with hundreds of materials — often layering and combining them — to create effects that felt original, tactile, and slightly surreal. To push those filters further, my camera assistants Michael “Moose” Belardi and Adam Gonzalez worked with gaffer Kevin Kim to build a custom matte box that allowed us to interact with the filters using external light. That flexibility gave us the ability to shape and activate the textures in camera, creating a visual language that felt playful, handcrafted, and specific to the film. Dir: Robb Boardman, DP: Markus Mentzer Category: Competition Format: 6K XOCN-LT Camera: Sony Burano Lenses: Sony G-Master Primes The Look: Simplicity and texture were the key principles that guided our choice of camera package. Robb and I knew early on that more than half the movie would be shot with a skeleton crew (without an AC). We also wanted the movie to be a love letter to LA, so capturing the city’s natural light was key. The Burano’s dynamic range is incredible, and its resolution and cutting-edge compression allowed us to bring back all that texture in post (thanks to Colorist Gabe Sanchez). Utilizing Sony G-Master Primes in conjunction with Black Satins and Hollywood Black Magic diffusion let us leverage Sony’s autofocus system, while also softening the appearance of lines and hard shadows. “Plantman” is an ensemble comedy inspired by thrillers like “Crimson Tide” and “Collateral,” where realism ultimately guides the story. Knowing that much of the movie would play in 2 and 3 shots, we found that a 2.20:1 aspect ratio gave us our widescreen feel, while still holding enough room for comedic performance. Dir: Vicky Jewson, DP: Bridger Nielson Category: Headliner Format: 4.6K ARRIRAW 2X Anamorphic Camera: ALEXA 35 Lenses: Vantage Hawk Class-X The Look: “Pretty Lethal” is a trip down the rabbit hole into a physical manifestation of the lost dreams of Uma Thurman’s character, Devora. For me, it was a rich opportunity to blend the two sides of Eastern Europe. This idea led to combining different eras of practical light fixtures into the design of our main location, a mafia den in rural Hungary. Upstairs would have the opulent chandeliers, candelabras, and goose-neck sconces, and the lower floors would have the late 20th-century retrofit with mixed fluorescent, sodium, and mercury fixtures with visible conduit. I like to remain open to new lens and body combinations to best serve the story we are trying to tell. We always knew that we wanted a classic 2.39:1 scope delivery, and so we tested several sets of 2x squeeze lenses on 3 different camera bodies. We shot a local Hungarian ballet dancer under a bunch of mixed lighting scenarios using all possible camera /lens combos. I was expecting the Hawk vintage 74’s to be the choice, but the Class-X on the A35 sensor at 1600 gave us a combo that felt organic, but also fantastical with just the right amount of flare characteristics to set that off-kilter, fairy-tale mood. This camera, lens, and practical lighting combo also rendered significant nuance in color on varied skin tones and beautiful fall-off. It lets us create a visual journey of different color contrasts and combinations coming from the fixtures you see in the picture, which mimics the journey of the characters through the story. Vicky and I originally bonded over a single image of 5 ballerinas crammed in a booth in an Eastern European diner. That image became our north star for the green peeling walls and pink lights of the bar. The mix of colors, fixtures, and trims let the space speak so much about who all the characters are and also be a place that could turn from welcoming to threatening as the troupe has to escape from the bowels of the inn. Dirs: Tyler Gillett and Matt Bettinelli-Olpin, DP: Brett Jutkiewicz Category: Headliner Format: 4.6K ARRIRAW Camera: ALEXA 35 Lenses: Cooke S5i The Look: As a sequel that begins immediately after the events of the first film, I wanted to keep the look consistent enough with the first film so that they could be watched together seamlessly, while still giving the sequel its own identity. I used the same lenses as the first film (Cooke S5i) but switched from the ALEXA Mini to the ALEXA 35, which I love for its exquisite color rendition, especially in skin tones, as well as its dynamic range and compact size. Shooting on spherical lenses allowed us to maintain close proximity to the actors and really put the audience within the action of the film, and the smaller camera body was great for all of the handheld and steadicam work we had throughout. My primary focus was to immerse the audience in Grace (Samara Weaving) and Faith’s (Kathryn Newton) journey, which often involved moving with the camera to underscore the intensity, tension, or suspense of each sequence. This film opens up the world from where the first ends, and the ALEXA 35 was great for handling all of our shooting environments from bright daylight to dark firelit rooms, and because I used a lot of mixed lighting to create color contrast and often to introduce a sense of unease to our interiors, it was important to have a camera that accurately captured the nuances of the lighting within the spaces. Dir: Gloria Mercer, DP: Devan Scott Category: Spotlight Format: 4K X-OCN, 6K Blackmagic Raw, and 4K X-AVC-HS Camera: Sony Venice (Super-35mm Mode), Blackmagic Pocket 6k (B-Cam), Sony A7IV (C/Crash Cam) Lenses: Fujinon Cabrio 19-90mm, Cooke S4 18/25/32/40/50/75/100mm, Super Takumar 50mm (W/ Tilt-Shift Adapter), Ironglass 58mm, Sigma 18-35mm, Sigma 24-70mm, Glimmerglass and Classic Soft filters. The Look: The look of “A Safe Distance” was heavily inspired by early New Hollywood genre cinema, and in particular the work of Vilmos Zsigmond. To that end, we employed an amusingly convoluted series of formulas involving an ever-changing mix of lens diffusion filters and film emulation looks. The goal was to progressively dirty up the image as the lead character leaves civilization for a world of wilderness and crime: the film starts in the realm of Kodak Vision3 500T and lightly applied Classic Soft filtration before gradually transitioning into a bleached-out mix of various expired Kodak and Fujifilm stocks and heavy Glimmerglass filtration. We ended up with about a dozen various permutations of filter strengths (we often stacked both types) that we chose on a scene-by-scene basis, and those were paired with eleven variations between the two extremes of our show look in the final colour grade. Our concerns with our camera and lens selections were largely practical: we needed a lightweight parfocal zoom lens that wouldn’t represent a burden in the forest, and so the Fujinon lens was a pragmatic choice. With it, we followed a rule on set: if the zoom lens was on the camera, I was allowed to improvise zooms on the spot at any point. This contributed to the film’s rough, fly-by-the-seat-of-one’s-pants aesthetic. The Venice was chosen as our A-camera for its low-light capabilities for shooting faux-firelight scenes, and the Blackmagic Pocket 6K and A7IV were on hand as lightweight B and crash cameras that matched easily to our A-cam. Digital tools, in particular, are central to my workflow: Being able to take the raw camera files home at night, bash them around in DaVinci Resolve, send out tests to the director and department heads, and create increasingly nuanced show LUTs as the shoot progresses has become a gigantic boon to our ability to experiment with the look of any given film in ever more radical ways. Dir: Lauren Noll, DP: Nathaniel Krause Category: Spotlight Format: 5K REDCODE RAW Camera: Two RED Gemini S35’s Lenses: Sigma Cine Primes and Sigma Cine Zooms The Look: For “Same Same But Different,” the camera package really grew out of the personality of the film itself. It’s a romantic comedy about growth, acceptance, and cultural collision — sometimes tender and introspective, sometimes completely unhinged. The tools had to feel as fluid as the characters and flexible enough to support the film’s wide emotional range. One of the first conversations Lauren and I had centered on whether we could realistically shoot with two cameras within our limited budget. Knowing she wanted the cast to have space to explore without sacrificing coverage, it made sense to build the production around my own gear: a RED Gemini paired with Sigma Cine lenses, with a duplicate package rented. Beyond logistics, however, the Gemini also felt right for the tonal scope of the story. Its dual native ISO gave us confidence moving from bright, sun-soaked beach sequences to intimate, low-light conversations without losing texture or subtlety. There’s a softness in the shadows that supports the vulnerability at the core of the film. The Sigma lenses complemented that tonal range beautifully — clean but not sterile, expressive without drawing attention to themselves. They are fairly fast, sharp, and neutral without being clinical, which gave us additional flexibility in post without committing to an overly baked-in look. Ultimately, the package aligned with the heart of the film: a story about identity caught between cultures, love entangled with circumstance, and friendship as the grounding force when everything else is in motion. The camera needed to hold both the absurdity of a green card wedding weekend and the intimacy of three women coming into their own — finding emotional truth in the middle of chaos. Dir: Kevin Hamedani, DP: Jon Keng Category: Spotlight Format: Sony 8.6K X-OCN Camera: Sony Venice 2 Lenses: Cineovision Anamorphics, Technovision Anamorphic Zooms, Atlas Orion Anamorphics The Look: Kevin and I were inspired by his favorite horror movies, in particular, John Carpenter’s “Halloween.” We wanted our cinematography to be bold and a modern tribute to Dean Cundey’s work. Much like “Halloween,” most of our film takes place in one house, and the lenses were crucial for creating visually arresting shots in otherwise ordinary locations. I chose the Cineovision/Technovision family specifically because of the zooms, which were important as voyeurism was one of the themes of the film. Old Fast Glass was able to hook us up with a stellar pair of Technovision zooms, which became the centerpiece of our storytelling. Dir: Aisha Evelyna, DP: Ian Carleton Category: Competition Format: Open Gate matted to 1.66:1 Camera: ALEXA Mini Lenses: Zeiss Super Speed MK III The Look: At this point in my indie film journey, everything is about getting the team more takes. The more time we can spend exploring together, the more in tune we can get with the director’s vision. On “Seahorse,” I chose to pull focus myself while operating, using an old-school manual follow focus and vintage glass. It’s an approach I’ve incorporated in my practice for years, both on narrative projects and in my daily Camera Roll photography zine, so it’s become second nature. Practically speaking, this means the resources of a focus puller can be shared with the team. An extra person on the art team or setting up lights can be the difference between two takes or three.
