But keeping this film sharp was unique. Aisha and I both knew the story required the core Toronto experience of biking through the hot sweaty city. Again, indie film, so no trailers or stabilized arms, but beyond that, we had an intentional visual language, part of it being handheld and intimate. A week before production, I made an all-or-nothing decision. Our PM lent me her roller skating pads and sourced a OneWheel (!) on a favour. On Day 1 of production, everyone nervously watching, I clipped into the EasyRig, took a wobbly first step onto the Onewheel, and Aisha and I rode off from the crew. As mentioned, you only get a handful of takes with indie films, so those first few riding shots are actually in the film, manual focus and all! This amalgamation of tools wonderfully captured the narrative. Biking through the city is a class story in itself, and I love how our working-class approach, which caused me at times to frantically weave with the main character, gifts the audience an authentic experience — process of creation driving art. While editing, Aisha casually noted there wasn’t a single take we had to scrap because of focus. That meant a lot to me. Dir: Victoria Strouse, DP: Tim Suhrstedt Category: Spotlight Format: 4.6K Super 35, 2.39:1 Aspect Ratio Camera: Sony Venice 2 Lenses: Angenieux Optimo Primes & Zooms The Look: As DP for Victoria Strouse, a writer and first-time director who wrote one of the best-structured, intelligent, and funny screenplays for an adult comedy that I’ve ever read, I wanted the film to feel naturalistic, fluid, and rich-looking. Our shooting schedule was only 20 days, with many locations, and driving shots (both practical and on an LED stage). For digital cinematography and for a theatrical feature, I try to make the images filmic. The Angenieux Optimo Primes are sharp but not harsh, have a low contrast, and are perfectly matched to the Optimo Zooms. We shot two cameras at all times, and, with the exception of night exteriors, we could have a zoom on one of the two cameras, which added some efficiency in terms of quick focal length changes. Victoria and I agreed that the film should be shot in the 2.39:1 aspect ratio. The story is about three adult siblings and another character on a road trip, and knowing that we would want shots with several characters in the shot, some of which are in a minivan, I knew that the wide-screen frame could accomplish this. Comedy plays very well in wide and medium shots, but the director and editor need coverage and choices in the editing room to fine-tune the rhythm of the film. You cannot be certain on the set which scenes play well in wide or medium shots, and which scenes need coverage to shape in the editing. A big part of a cinematographer’s job is to give the director the best selection of shots to work with. Dir: Russell Goldman, DP: Gemma Doll-Grossman Category: Competition Format: X-OCN-ST 4K Camera: 2x Sony Venice 1 (Rialto mode), GoPro Hero 12 Black Lenses: Standard Baltars, Laowa 12mm, Angenieux 18.5mm, Laowa periscope probe The Look: Cardboard boxes dictated the cinematography of “Sender.” Our protagonist Julia (Britt Lower) is three weeks sober. She’s doing lots of online shopping, but starts to receive unwanted items that soon trigger painful memories and a growing sense that she is being watched. With each new package, Julia’s paranoia spikes. Cardboard has never been so fraught. Director Russell Goldman and I sought to craft a visual approach that feels like the perspective of a hidden (but imaginary) camera. The high-angle/low-angle, omniscient perspectives are symptomatic of Julia’s paranoiac worldview. The camera remains stubbornly static (No pans! No tilts!), unless it’s rigged to an everyday object — a tube of lipstick, a pill bottle, a cardboard box — which offers the impression of surveillance. Shout-out to Danny, Tim, Max, Brandon, Sophie, and Bianca at Becine and the art crew for troubleshooting all of these rigs. The collaboration was truly interdepartmental. Naturally, we opted for Sony Venice cameras in Rialto mode, because the camera is both excellent quality and compact. Our lenses are similarly compact (Standard Baltars) and, when wide open, get funky and hazy, just like Julia’s psyche. Most of the film is captured with 12mm and 18.5mm lenses, which emulated a security camera’s perspective and gave our protagonist a wide canvas to play. And, undoubtedly, our aspect ratio is boxy, 3:2. Dirs: Daisy Rosato, Nora Kaye, DP: Daisy Rosato Category: Visions Format: 4K ProRes Camera: iPhone 15 Pro Lenses: Moment 19mm lens for principle photography; native iPhone lens for underwater photography The Look: Shooting an improvised feature in six days requires delusion and a swiftness in all aspects, and the camera was no different. Deciding that the iPhone would be our camera was one of the first decisions Nora and I made. It’s an homage to the lo-fi cameras of the Dogme 95 and mumblecore/gore movements, but, like those, it was also a financial decision. Not only is the iPhone’s price point lower than traditional cinema cameras, but its smaller footprint lends itself to experimentation. We were able to dive into the ocean, mount to a bike, shoot guerrilla scenes in NYC without being spotted, put the camera in a box, the freezer, the shower… You name it, we probably put the camera in it. “Sinner Supper Club” is a film about the disintegration of a friend group, about the things and people that haunt us, and the messiness of community. Shooting this way also put me directly into the fray of our characters’ psychology; I was able to move quickly, keeping up as our actors made discoveries through improvisation. The result is a visceral and immersive experience, unafraid of the iPhone’s rougher edges and our own. People loooove to say, “You have an iPhone, just shoot your movie!” so we did. Dir: Jenna MacMillan, DP: Kevin A Fraser Category: Competition Format: 4.6K 3:2 Open Gate Prores 4444 Camera: ALEXA 35 Lenses: DZO Pavo 2X Anamorphic, Hawk modified Angenieux 48-580mm The Look: From the images Jenna responded to in our earliest meetings, it was apparent that anamorphic glass would provide the right character for “The Snake.” We didn’t want the lenses to be optically perfect; they needed to have flaws and imperfections like our characters in the film. Many of the character anamorphics I’ve used, like the Lomos & Hawks, have a somewhat far minimum focus distance. We wanted to do long, dynamic takes where the camera is dancing, with the performers at times moving very close to them, so having to cut to put in a diopter was not going to fly. Searching for the right lenses, I found the DZO Pavos, which I had not previously known. They were fairly new at the time, and it was hard to get our hands on a set. Through creative means, we ended up with a mixed set of lenses coming from several vendors. Some were the blue flare versions, others the neutral flare. This was a happy accident for me; the mixed set of relatively cheap, lightweight lenses lent to the scrappy punk-rock aesthetic of the film and our approach to shooting it. We also had a Hawk modified 48-580mm Angenieux zoom because we wanted to shoot select scenes from a far distance with a huge amount of lens compression. I felt this made it feel like Jamie was both being observed/judged by outsiders and that she was somewhat trapped in her situation at those times in the film. The ALEXA 35 was an easy choice, and we didn’t seriously consider any other body. I’d shot several movies with it previously and knew it could handle any dynamic range, and the ISO is flexible without much image sacrifice. It’s important to me to not have to think too much about the camera during production, it frees me up to be more present with the director & cast so I can focus on the heart of each scene. Dir: Fergus Campbell, DP: Keldon Duane-McGlashan Category: Spotlight Format: ARRIRAW 4K 16:9 Camera: ALEXA 35 Lenses: Bausch & Lomb Super Baltars, Tokina Cinema 11-20mm T2.9 The Look: Director Fergus Campbell and I wanted to create a visual language that captured the stillness of a teenage summer in Reno while hinting at the film’s magical realism. The Super Baltars provided a ton of character — so much so that shooting wide open was reserved for a select few scenes where soft, hazy edges complemented the story. We established early in prep that we would avoid excessively shallow depth of field, quick cuts, and instead shoot long, wide takes at a higher f-stop to capture the ensemble cast in reaction to one another. The Baltars maintained their character even as we stopped up, perfect for daytime desert scenes where we desired clarity in the rich backgrounds. We shot on my ALEXA 35, utilizing the soft nostalgic grain setting and, of course, relying on its dynamic range for bright exteriors. Gaffer Nica Fazio and Grip Ryan Moore were beyond incredible, testing their expertise against sand, hail, rain, and mud. Every person on this “Sparks” crew worked so hard to bring the movie to life, and for that I am forever grateful! Dir: Joe Swanberg, DP: Eon Mora Category: Spotlight Format: 35mm Camera: Panavision Millenium XL2, DJI Inspire 3 Lenses: Panavision Primo Classic Series Primes The Look: I have always been fond of these lenses as workhorses for the 35mm format. I feel like the represent as the high-water mark for Panavision’s engineering, as well as retaining a ton of character without loss of sharpness. Bringing the Alaskan landscape into the image was a major priority for this project, so choosing a lens that could reproduce the naturalism we were going for, without editorializing too much, was a goal. I love the way that they flair, something that plays a major role in the imagery of this film, with the perpetual sunlight throughout. They offer a simple but profoundly organic look that I really appreciate. Dir: Stella Marie Markert, DPs: Edgar Fischnaller and Jonas Kolahdoozan Category: Global Format: 4.8k Open Gate ProRes4444 Camera: ALEXA 35 Lenses: Zeiss Standard Speeds, Canon 16mm Zoom Lens The Look: Authenticity guided every decision in our cinematography. The film follows four girls sharing a flat in Berlin’s Prenzlauer Berg, each with a vibrant and distinct personality. From the beginning, it was important that the visual language would never overshadow these characters. Our goal was to create a camera style that feels present and alive while allowing the performances and relationships to remain at the center of the film. We shot on Zeiss Standard Speed lenses and relied largely on natural lighting to achieve an image that feels organic and unpolished. The character of the lenses complemented the emotional honesty of the story and supported the sometimes chaotic energy of the four protagonists without imposing a heavy visual style. The camera is mostly handheld and constantly moving, subtly breathing with the characters and the rhythm of their interactions. Rather than observing from a distance, the camera exists within their shared space. As the story progresses and the bond between the girls deepens, the camera gradually moves closer. Early in the film, our close-ups remain slightly wider, maintaining a sense of distance. As trust grows between the characters, the camera becomes more intimate, reflecting their emotional closeness. For the sequences exploring the characters’ childhoods, we wanted a visual distinction without changing the aspect ratio. We imagined these moments as point-and-shoot photographs from a summer vacation. To create a flatter and more two-dimensional quality, we used the ALEXA 35 in its 16mm mode paired with a 16mm zoom lens. Dir: Katie Aselton, DP: Sarah Whelden Category: Spotlight Format: 6K RAW Lite Camera: Canon C400 & C80 Lenses: Canon CN-E The Look: My first conversation with director Katie Aselton centered around building an intimate set with an extremely minimal footprint, creating a space as raw and vulnerable as the story we were telling, and allowing us to pivot quickly if inspiration struck. Practically speaking, I needed a camera package that was flexible enough to handle the plethora of locations, including all of the expansive night exteriors, but that was small and nimble enough to not slow down our extremely small team. We landed on two Canon C400s for our handheld and tripod rigs, with a C80 setup full-time on a small gimbal. I already loved Canon’s amazing color science and knew they would be a great fit ergonomically for the films, but the triple base ISO proved to be a game-changer for me. I loved how quickly I could shift my dynamic range for a consistent look as we moved around the streets of Bangor, Maine, with very little lighting equipment. The CN-Es gave us a very modern look with beautiful flares and just enough character when opened up. Their wide aperture allowed us to really isolate our actors, letting the world fade away as their relationship with each other blossoms throughout the film. Dir: Emily Robinson, DP: Brody S. Anderson Category: Spotlight Format: 4.6K ProRes Camera: ALEXA 35 Lenses: Zeiss Super Speeds, Angenieux 35-140mm The Look: Emily — as the writer, director, and lead — had a very clear sense of the story she wanted to share, so I felt my function was to just help her tell it as efficiently as possible. Knowing we were going to be tight on time, we devised a simple shooting strategy and assembled a camera package that let us zip through our days. We landed on the ALEXA 35 combined with Zeiss Super Speeds, which allowed our gaffer, Paul Kim, and me to use a mix of natural light and smaller sources without compromising the exposure we wanted. Emily and I believed a few key scenes needed to be long, uninterrupted zooms, but I didn’t want the burden of a heavy lens, so Dan Thomas and Kevin Kenney at Eastside Camera pointed us to an old Angeniuex zoom they call Cricket. It weighs about 2lbs and has a great range. It paired nicely with the primes, and its tiny size allowed our first and only AC, Riona Oshima-Ryan (a godsend), to swap back and forth quickly. Honestly, I’m not so sure the gear had a huge impact on the film. It all helped, of course, but I think equipment gets a little overemphasized, and I’d love to shift attention back to Emily, the cast, and the crew. People make movies. Thanks for spending a sweaty spring in Toluca Lake with me! Dir: Graham Parkes, DP: Christopher Ripley Category: Competition Format: Sony X-OCN Camera: Sony Venice 2 Lenses: Zeiss Super Speeds, Compact Angenieux Optimo Zooms The Look: It was really important for our world to feel alive and messy. Although the film plays with fantastical ideas, the emotional throughline is very human, so we wanted our visual language to be grounded. Zeiss Super Speeds and compact Angenieux Optimos were perfect lenses for that. They’re lightweight, which was great for our handheld shooting style, and they tend to render images with a straightforward functionality that feels lived-in instead of commercially clean or overly vintage and affected. My crew lit close to 360-degree sets, mainly using practicals and a few small hidden units. We wanted the actors to have the freedom to move around and inhabit the space fully, and for our camera to be able to explore new angles and lively moments with every take — all while maintaining our shooting pace and avoiding relighting. The director Graham and I shamelessly called it “Supermarket Sweep:” A hungry, chaotic, playful camera that strove to capture lightning in a bottle, even with our fast 20-day shooting schedule. We shot Sony Venice 2, which I think has a lovely color science and rendering of detail. With the right film emulation and color pipeline, the Venice 2 has a transparent quality to it that feels more like a photochemical print from the late ‘90s or early 2000s. That “ungraded 35mm” image felt well-suited to our grounded style of storytelling. By providing your information, you agree to our Terms of Use and our Privacy Policy. Title: Meta weighing massive 20% workforce layoffs in effort to offset AI infrastructure costs: report
URL: https://www.foxbusiness.com/technology/meta-eyes-massive-20-workforce-cut-ai-infrastructure-costs-continue-soar-across-operations-report
Time Published: 2026-03-14T14:26:51Z
Full Content:
Evercore ISI senior managing director Mark Mahaney breaks down his stock picks on 'Varney & Co.' Meta is reportedly weighing layoffs that could impact at least 20% of its workforce as the tech giant looks to offset rising artificial intelligence costs. The cuts come as the technology company aims to offset the cost of artificial intelligence infrastructure and prepare for greater efficiency brought about by AI-assisted workers, three sources familiar with the matter told Reuters. The outlet added that the timing and size of the potential layoffs have not been finalized. When reached for comment, a Meta spokesperson told FOX Business, "This is a speculative report about theoretical approaches." META CUTS OVER 1,000 JOBS IN MAJOR METAVERSE RETREAT Meta CEO Mark Zuckerberg arrives at the Los Angeles Superior Court at United States Court House on Feb. 18, 2026, in Los Angeles, California. (Jill Connelly/Getty Images / Getty Images) According to Reuters, top Meta executives recently shared plans for the proposed layoffs with other senior leaders at the company. If the company were to slash 20% of its employees, the layoffs would amount to Meta's largest restructuring since 2022 and early 2023, the outlet said. Meta laid off 11,000 workers in November 2022 — around 13% of its workforce at the time, Reuters reported. The company cut another 10,000 jobs months later. JUDGE BLOCKS META FROM INTRODUCING 'EXAGGERATED' CLAIMS IN SOCIAL MEDIA TRIAL Meta is reportedly considering layoffs that could affect up to 20% of its workforce as the company invests heavily in artificial intelligence infrastructure. (David Paul Morris/Bloomberg via Getty Images / Getty Images) Meta employed nearly 79,000 people as of Dec. 31, according to its latest filing. Other major companies, including Amazon, have recently announced large-scale layoffs tied to AI developments. In January, Amazon cut around 16,000 jobs and signaled at the time that more reductions could follow. (Getty Images / Getty Images) The company previously announced a first round of cuts totaling about 14,000 white-collar layoffs in October, bringing its corporate reductions to roughly 30,000 roles. In making the cuts, which represented nearly 10% of its white-collar workforce, Amazon cited efficiency gains from artificial intelligence and broader cultural changes. Title: Meta planning sweeping layoffs as AI costs mount
URL: https://economictimes.indiatimes.com/tech/technology/exclusive-meta-planning-sweeping-layoffs-as-ai-costs-mount/articleshow/129567894.cms
Time Published: 2026-03-14T02:44:49Z
Full Content:
Listen to this article in summarized format (Catch all the Technology News News, and Latest News Updates on The Economic Times.) ...more Popular Categories Hot on Web In Case you missed it Top Searched Companies Other useful Links Top Calculators Top Slideshow Top Story Listing Top Prime Articles Top Definitions Top Commodities Private Companies Top Market Pages Latest News follow us on Download ET App:

Title: Meta layoffs: Mark Zuckerberg's tech firm plans to cut nearly 16,000 jobs as AI costs mount, says report — what we know
URL: https://www.livemint.com/companies/news/meta-layoffs-mark-zuckerbergs-tech-firm-plans-to-cut-nearly-16-000-jobs-as-ai-costs-mount-says-report-what-we-know-11773451516319.html
Time Published: 2026-03-14T02:10:44Z
Full Content:
Mark Zuckerberg-led Meta is planning sweeping layoffs that could affect 20% or more of the company to offset costly artificial intelligence infrastructure bets and prepare for greater efficiency brought about by AI-assisted workers, according to a Reuters report. However, no date has been set for these cuts, and the magnitude has not been finalised, Reuters was told by sources. Top executives have recently signaled the plans to other senior leaders at Meta and told them to begin planning how to pare back, anonymous sources told Reuters. "This is speculative reporting about theoretical approaches," Meta spokesperson Andy Stone said in response to questions about the plan. Meta employed nearly 79,000 people as of December 31, according to its latest filing. If Meta settles on the 20% figure, this layoff may affect nearly 16,000 employees, making it the most significant job cut at Mark Zuckerberg's company since its restructuring in the "year of efficiency" — late 2022 and early 2023. Meta laid off 11,000 staffers in November 2022, around 13% of its workforce at the time. Around four months later, it announced it was cutting another 10,000 jobs. Over the last year, CEO Zuckerberg has been pushing Meta to compete more forcefully in generative AI. The company has offered huge pay packages, some worth hundreds of millions of dollars over four years, to court top AI researchers to a new superintelligence team. The company has said it plans to invest $600 billion to build data centres by 2028. Earlier this week, it acquired Moltbook, a social networking platform built for AI agents. Meta is also spending at least $2 billion to buy a Chinese AI startup – Manus. Zuckerberg has alluded to efficiency gains from the investments, saying in January he was starting to see "projects that used to require big teams now be accomplished by a single very talented person." Meta's planned AI investments follow a series of setbacks with its Llama 4 models last year, including criticism that it provided misleading results on the benchmarks it used for early versions. It abandoned the release of the largest version of that model, called Behemoth, which had been due out in the summer. Meta's superintelligence team has been working to reassert the company's standing this year by building a new model called Avocado, but the performance of that model has also lagged expectations. Its release has also been pushed to later. Meta's layoff plans reflect a broader pattern among major US companies, particularly in tech, this year. Executives have pointed to recent improvements in AI systems as one reason for the changes. In January, Amazon confirmed it would cut some 16,000 jobs, amounting to nearly 10% of its workforce. Last month, the fintech company Block chopped nearly half of its staff, with CEO Jack Dorsey explicitly pointing to AI tools and their growing capability to help companies do more with smaller teams. (With Reuters inputs) Catch all the Business News , Corporate news , Breaking News Events and Latest News Updates on Live Mint. Download The Mint News App to get Daily Market Updates. Download the Mint app and read premium stories

Title: Exclusive-Meta planning sweeping layoffs as AI costs mount
URL: https://www.channelnewsasia.com/business/exclusive-meta-planning-sweeping-layoffs-ai-costs-mount-5993031
Time Published: 2026-03-14T00:19:19Z
Full Content:
Business If Meta settles on the 20 per cent figure, the layoffs will be its most significant since a restructuring in late 2022 and early 2023 that it dubbed the "year of efficiency". People near a Meta sign outside of the company's headquarters in Menlo Park, California, Mar 7, 2023. Last month, the fintech company Block chopped nearly half of its staff, with CEO Jack Dorsey explicitly pointing to AI tools and their growing capability to help companies do more with smaller teams. Meta's planned AI investments follow a series of setbacks with its Llama 4 models last year, including criticism that it provided misleading results on the benchmarks it used for early versions. It abandoned the release of the largest version of that model, called Behemoth, which had been due out in the summer.The superintelligence team has been working to reassert the company's standing this year by building a new model called Avocado, but the performance of that model has also lagged expectations. The sources spoke anonymously because they were not authorised to disclose the cuts. "This is speculative reporting about theoretical approaches," Meta spokesperson Andy Stone said in response to questions about the plan. If Meta settles on the 20 per cent figure, the layoffs will be the company's most significant since a restructuring in late 2022 and early 2023 that it dubbed the "year of efficiency". It employed nearly 79,000 people as of Dec 31, according to its latest filing. The company laid off 11,000 staffers in November 2022, or around 13 per cent of its workforce at the time. Around four months later, it announced it was cutting another 10,000 jobs. Over the last year, CEO Mark Zuckerberg has been pushing Meta to compete more forcefully in generative AI. The company has offered huge pay packages, some worth hundreds of millions of dollars over four years, to court top AI researchers to a new superintelligence team. The company has said it plans to invest US$600 billion to build data centres by 2028. Earlier this week, it acquired Moltbook, a social networking platform built for AI agents. Meta is also spending at least US$2 billion to buy Chinese AI startup Manus, Reuters previously reported. Zuckerberg has alluded to efficiency gains from the investments, saying in January he was starting to see "projects that used to require big teams now be accomplished by a single very talented person". Meta's plans reflect a broader pattern among major US companies, particularly in tech, this year. Executives have pointed to recent improvements in AI systems as one reason for the changes. In January, Amazon confirmed it would cut some 16,000 jobs, amounting to nearly 10 per cent of its workforce. Last month, the fintech company Block chopped nearly half of its staff, with CEO Jack Dorsey explicitly pointing to AI tools and their growing capability to help companies do more with smaller teams. Meta's planned AI investments follow a series of setbacks with its Llama 4 models last year, including criticism that it provided misleading results on the benchmarks it used for early versions. It abandoned the release of the largest version of that model, called Behemoth, which had been due out in the summer. The superintelligence team has been working to reassert the company's standing this year by building a new model called Avocado, but the performance of that model has also lagged expectations. Title: Meta planning sweeping layoffs as AI costs mount
URL: https://www.channelnewsasia.com/business/meta-layoffs-artificial-intelligence-tech-zuckerberg-facebook-5993031
Time Published: 2026-03-14T00:19:00Z
Full Content:
Business If Meta settles on the 20 per cent figure, the layoffs will be its most significant since a restructuring in late 2022 and early 2023 that it dubbed the "year of efficiency". People near a Meta sign outside of the company's headquarters in Menlo Park, California, Mar 7, 2023. (File photo: AP/Jeff Chiu) NEW YORK/SAN FRANCISCO: Meta is planning sweeping layoffs that could affect 20 per cent or more of the company, three sources familiar with the matter told Reuters. This comes as Meta seeks to offset costly artificial intelligence infrastructure bets and prepare for greater efficiency brought about by AI-assisted workers. No date has been set for the cuts and the magnitude has not been finalised, the people said. Top executives have recently signalled the plans to other senior leaders at Meta and told them to begin planning how to pare back, two of the people said. If Meta settles on the 20 per cent figure, the layoffs will be the company's most significant since a restructuring in late 2022 and early 2023 that it dubbed the "year of efficiency". It employed nearly 79,000 people as of Dec 31, according to its latest filing.The company laid off 11,000 staffers in November 2022, or around 13 per cent of its workforce at the time.
